content:
Hi,
I liked it, though I was a bit slow.
I initially assumed 'X's would fill up to 1st 8 multiple >=c, the first test case dissipating the ambiguity is the 13th 'Natural Beauty', that's why I would set c to something >16 in 7th 'Not enough character', unless that was intended.

IMO this is a suitable Reverse clash; pattern recognition was clear and simple to spot. Feel free to enable Fastest/Shortest modes if you want :)

Some preliminary comments from me:

- Suggestion: include test cases where length and/or characters is 0? Not sure how suitable that would be for this clash.

- Please add some test cases where the required number of characters is a multiple of 8 and is not equal to the length of the string.

- To balance out the number of test cases where the output has trailing Xs in the bottom left corner of the grid, please add / modify some test cases to have trailing Xs in the bottom right corner of the grid.

Also for reviewers: there exists a related but not too similar clash: https://www.codingame.com/contribute/view/126504864e7133e8ebd16e670b7e00f3797c70

This is temporarily a Reverse puzzle only just so I can see if the pattern is too easy or too hard to spot. Both beginners and veterans are welcome to give it a try, and if you have time, please give me feedback on the pattern recognition speed. Was it instant, did it take 0-3 minutes, did it take 3-7 minutes, or could you not recognize it at all. Once I've gotten a good idea of how this puzzle might do in Reverse, then I'll enable Fastest, add a statement, and decide whether to keep it or not.

Also if I'm missing any edge cases (if you can even spot any), feel free to alert me about it, and I'll happily take a look at it.

(and most importantly, PLEASE DO NOT DOWNVOTE JUST BECAUSE REVERSE MIGHT BE TOO HARD; I will add a statement later, don't worry :)
Do you want to draw reviewers' attention that the stub generator contains a hint?

Thanks for the feedback @User123! I've added 2 sets of test cases, one where the number is longer but the character count is a multiple of 8, and one where the number's length is a multiple of 8, but the character count is not and is also larger. About some of your other suggestions:

** - Suggestion: include test cases where length and/or characters is 0? Not sure how suitable that would be for this clash. **
--> Currently, my solution can't handle cases where the character count is 0. Plus, I feel like that just leads to more edge cases that players would need to look out for. Just like you said, I don't think it would be very suitable.

**- Please add some test cases where the required number of characters is a multiple of 8 and is not equal to the length of the string.**
--> DONE! (eg. Forcing Perfection and VD: Forcing No X's)

**- To balance out the number of test cases where the output has trailing Xs in the bottom left corner of the grid, please add / modify some test cases to have trailing Xs in the bottom right corner of the grid.**
--> It was just unbalanced by one case, so I've modified the last case to make them balanced.

**Also for reviewers: there exists a related but not too similar clash: https://www.codingame.com/...**

--> Mine should be unique enough to be its own idea, right? That clash had numbers counting up, while mine just read the digits off of a number. Additionally, there's also the "X" gap fillers too that the other clash didn't have. And lastly, this is a horizontal matrix too, while the other was a vertical one.

Thanks for the feedback @k4rl!

I'm really sorry to say this, but your comment was a bit hard to understand. However, what I think I've grasped out of your message was that setting c to a value >16 in "Not Enough Characters" can make the pattern easier to recognize. So I've changed c to 17 for that case. Would that make the pattern easier to spot in any way?

Perfect, thanks.
Trying to rephrase if that makes any more sense:
Up to test 13 it was unknown, when c>l, whether X padding was up to (l+7)//8*l or (c+7)//8*c.
Since test 7 was named 'Not enough character', it seemed fit to exhibit this behavior (first test with c>l) there than later.
Sorry, I pointed out the other clash to show that yours is sufficiently unique. All's good. Thanks for making the changes!
Oh good point! I'll remove those hints for the meantime to test the strength of the pattern, but if the pattern is impossible to spot for some players, then I'll add them back.
Well, there are also the clash title and the test case titles to consider, if you're going for a completely hint-free experience.
I don't think the test case titles really give away too much on the pattern, but I've updated the title. (I really want those titles when I enable fastest mode, too!)

Hi @5DN1L! I've added my statement and enabled Fastest mode. Could you just check to make sure that my statement is fine before I send this clash as Public. Additionally, I figured out how to remove the length part of the stub, and when I had this idea, I never intended for the length of the string to be part of the input. Is that removal fine with you?

Thanks!

Hi! Thank you for the update.

Regarding your second question, I'd say that the string length is optional and I don't usually request it as part of the input in similar scenarios. That said, including it is still good practice, as it helps participants  using languages like C determine the exact memory allocation needed.

As for the clash:

Constraints
* Please remove the 3rd and 4th constraints:

  • The 3rd constraint repeats information already specified in the Input section.

  • The 4th constraint describes an output requirement rather than a true constraint.

Cases
* You might want to adjust one pair of test cases so that the string begins with one or more leading zeros. This helps ensure participants handle such cases correctly. While it's unlikely that someone would convert the string to an integer (thereby dropping leading zeros), it's still a useful edge case to include.

Thanks.

Thanks for the feedback @5DN1L! I've added a test case with leading Zeros and one with trailing zeros too. (totally not my OCD, trust me ;)

I was a bit confused about your answer to the string length question as you seemed to support both sides. However, you did say it was optional but just recommended, and I think that it's okay to not keep it because I already have the length constraint anyway. If there's nothing else, then just give me the "all clear", and I can change this to "Ready"

> it was optional but just recommended
Yup, that's what I meant.

I've got no further comments at the moment 🙂
